Title: Structure of In Vitro-Synthesized Cellulose Fibrils Viewed by Cryo-Electron Tomography and 13C Natural-Abundance Dynamic Nuclear Polarization Solid-State NMR

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: polymerizes UDP-glucose into β-1,4-glucan chains that assemble into cellulose microfibrils
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: structural cell wall biomaterial; central renewable material source
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: overexpressed in Physcomitrium patens material used to prepare membrane fractions
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: in vitro incubation of membrane fractions for 24 h at room temperature with cellobiose and UDP-glucose; negative control lacked UDP-glucose and cellobiose
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: other CESA proteins in cellulose synthase complexes/microsomal membrane fraction (not specifically validated in this paper)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: produces in vitro-synthesized cellulose microfibrils whose structure closely resembles native plant cell wall cellulose and is consistent with an 18-chain model with limited bundling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: in vitro synthesis using membrane fractions from P. patens overexpressing HA-tagged CESA5; negative-control substrate omission; TEM/cryo-electron tomography; subtomogram averaging; DNP solid-state NMR; spectral deconvolution
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Membrane fractions from Physcomitrium patens overexpressing CESA5 synthesized abundant cellulose microfibrils in vitro, whereas substrate-omission controls did not. 2. The resulting fibrils showed periodic wrapped sub-fibril architecture and dimensions consistent with plant cellulose microfibrils. 3. DNP-NMR demonstrated that the in vitro product retained native-like cellulose conformations and glucan-chain distributions consistent with an 18-chain cellulose model, with only minimal bundling.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: supports use of CESA5-containing in vitro systems for engineering cellulose-based biomaterials and for mechanistic studies of cellulose microfibril assembly; useful for tuning fibril structure rather than a direct crop nutrition breeding target
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This paper is primarily a structural characterization study, not a gene-function dissection of nutrient metabolism. CESA5 is the only experimentally central gene/protein, and the metabolite/product is cellulose rather than a nutritional quality metabolite.
